Safety action is initiated if the power level exceeds 11.25 Mw or if the reactor period becomes shorter than 1 sec.

The MSRE has two modes of control: for operation at power levels less than 1 Mw, the flux (or power level) is the controlled variable; for operation at power levels of 1 Mw or higher, the fuel salt temperature at the reactor core outlet is the controlled variable.
The performance of the reactor control system under the temperature mode of control at power levels of 1 Mw and above (Figs.
4 and 5) and the neutron flux mode of control at power levels below 1 Mw (Figs.
6 and 7) was demonstrated.
4, the reactor was initially operating at steady-state full power (7.5 Mw); and at the time marked "start" in Fig.
4, the temperature set point on the controller $(T_{\text{so}})$ was decreased at a constant rate of $5^{\circ}\text{F} / \text{min}$ for a total time of 2.5 min (12.5°F total).
5, when in the run illustrated by this figure the heat load, or heat rejection rate, was suddenly reduced from 7.5 to $\sim 5$ Mw.
6 and 7, the controller was in the flux mode of control, and the reactor was operating at a steady-state power level of $10\mathrm{kw}$ .
A perturbation was induced in the control system by suddenly reducing the flux set point on the controller from 10 to $8\mathrm{kw}$ .

10 were that the reactor was $0.04 \, \text{K} / \text{K}$ subcritical at a power level of $0.01 \, \text{W}$ and the gamma activity level
was equivalent to a neutron flux of $100\mathrm{kw}$ .
For this case, the shortest reactor period was approximately 0.15 sec, and the peak power was approximately $630\mathrm{kw}$ .
The $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ resuspension accident is only a postulated accident, and whether or not a mechanism for its happening exists is debatable.
During operation of the reactor, a fraction of the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ would precipitate from the fuel solution and collect in the primary loop external to the reactor core.
After a substantial mass of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ has collected in the primary loop outside the reactor core, it could be swept back through the core by the flowing fuel solution.
As the highly concentrated $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ mass traversed the reactor core, the reactivity would increase, causing a power excursion.

As an example, while the maximum integrated amount of reactivity removed from the core in this process may have been about $1\% \delta K / K$ , the maximum postulated reactivity increase on resuspension may approximate $5\% \delta K / K$ .
Startup Accident, Starting $0.048\mathrm{K} / \mathrm{K}$ Subcritical and with Gamma Background Ten Times Normal.
The relative reactivity of the mass of resuspended $^{23}$ U as a function of time is shown in Fig.
11, in which the mass of resuspended $^{23}$ U enters the reactor core at time zero.
11 was set up on a diode function generator.

The output of the function generator (relative reactivity) was multiplied by the reactivity equivalent of the mass of $^{23}$ U deposited outside the core ( $\mathsf{SK}_{\circ}$ ), and the result was the reactivity as a function of time.
The total reactivity change was $-4\%$ $\delta K / K$ .

This was the end of the incident for the cases where the instantaneous reactivity due to the resuspended $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ did not exceed $+4\% \delta \mathrm{K} / \mathrm{K}$ (the corresponding $5\mathrm{K}_{0}$ is approximately $+0.82\% \delta \mathrm{K} / \mathrm{K}$ ).

If $5\mathrm{K}_{0}$ would exceed $+0.82\% \delta \mathrm{K} / \mathrm{K}$ , the resulting positive reactivity would more than compensate for the $-4\% \delta \mathrm{K} / \mathrm{K}$ due to safety rod motion, and the reactor would go critical again.
The strip chart recordings of three runs in which the only parameter varied was $\delta K_{0}$ are shown in Figs.
The initial power level for each of these runs was $7.5\mathrm{Mw}$ .
With a value of $+0.75\%$ $\delta K / K$ for $\delta K_{0}$ (Fig.
13), the safety rod motion drove the reactor subcritical, and it remained subcritical with essentially no temperature increases.
With $\delta K_{0}$ equal to $+1\%$ $\delta K / K$ (Fig.
14) the system temperatures did increase significantly.
15 with $\delta K_{0}$ for the run equal to $+1.2\%$ $\delta K / K$ .

The existing controller will control the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ fueled system in a stable manner; however, an increased velocity feedback gain will be required.
The safety system limited the startup accident so that the peak power was only $100\mathrm{kw}$ (10 times normal gamma background).

A quantity of $233\mathrm{U}$ sufficient to cause a reactivity change of approximately $-1\% \delta K / K$ when precipitated out of the fuel solution at some point in the system external to the core could be swept back into the core in a concentrated form without causing excessive core damage.
The maximum allowable fuel temperature increase at the hot channel would be $340^{\circ}F$ .

The case where $\delta K_{o} = 1\% \delta K / K$ was conservatively chosen as the limiting case.

The input to $3l_{2}$ is the output of $2l_{3} - 2T_{c, in}^{\prime} + 112.5$ .
The quantity 112.5 is simply two times the desired scaled voltage value of the core outlet temperature, or two times the core outlet set point, $T_{so}^{\prime}$ .
The output of $3l_{2}$ is then
1 0 \left[ 1 / 5 \left(T _ {c, \text {o u t}} ^ {\prime L} - T _ {s o} ^ {\prime}\right) - 1 / 5 \left(T _ {c, i n} ^ {\prime L} - T _ {c, i n} ^ {\prime}\right) \right] - 5 P ^ {L}. \tag {43}
Amplifier $32_{2}$ simply inverts the signal so that its output is the same as the above but multiplied by $-1$ .
The input of $30_{2}$ is the output of $32_{2}$ minus $5P$ .
We consider the output of $30_{2}$ to be kinetic error signal $\varepsilon_{K}$ , with
\varepsilon_ {K} = 2 \left(T _ {c, \text {o u t}} ^ {\prime L} - T _ {s o} ^ {\prime}\right) - 2 \left(T _ {c, i n} ^ {\prime L} - T _ {c, i n} ^ {\prime}\right) + 5 (P - P ^ {L}). \tag {44}
(44) are, in effect, time derivatives averaged over 100 sec.
The only persistent condition for $\varepsilon_{K} = 0$ , therefore, appears to be the one when all three terms vanish.
\varepsilon = 2 \varepsilon_ {K} - v \tag {45}
Gains are so chosen that this feedback is comparable to the signal produced by a small fluctuation in temperature, say of the order of $1^{\circ}$ .
The power-level control circuit, which is used at power levels below 1 Mw where temperature control is neither practical nor desirable, is much simpler.
The connection between $32_{2}$ and $30_{2}$ is broken, and amplifiers $21_{3}, 31_{2}$ , and $32_{2}$ are simply not used.
In their place a positive voltage (whose value is chosen by the operator) is fed into $30_{2}$ .
Hence, the input to $30_{2}$ is $5(\mathsf{P}_{\mathsf{SO}} - \mathsf{P})$ .
We then have at the output of $30_{2}$ :
\varepsilon_ {K} = 5 \left(P - P _ {s o}\right). \tag {46}
